FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study) at baseline and 1-year follow-up, N=9,066 children aged 9-10 primarily.

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific smartphone/SM variables: social screen time (social media, texting, video chatting) and nonsocial screen time (YouTube, video games) separately on weekdays and weekends.

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports inferential statistical tests (logistic regression with odds ratios) linking psychiatric symptoms as predictors to screen time outcomes one year later, controlled for covariates. Also tests reverse direction: screen time predicting future psychiatric symptoms. Examples: OR=1.22 (95% CI 1.22-1.23) for clinical total psychiatric problems predicting weekday nonsocial screen time; OR=1.15 (95% CI 1.10-1.12) for higher social screen time predicting future ADHD.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple odds ratios reported in text, tables, and figures with 95% confidence intervals throughout. Examples: OR=1.22, 95% CI 1.22-1.23; OR=1.65, 95% CI 1.63-1.67; OR=1.15, 95% CI 1.10-1.12.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis: longitudinal prospective design comparing baseline psychiatric symptoms to screen time patterns one year later, with reciprocal analyses.

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in Social Psychiatry and Psychiatric Epidemiology (2024), not a preprint, conference abstract, or duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply because the paper reports analyses in BOTH directions: (1) psychiatric symptoms (individual clinical characteristics) as predictors of screen time, AND (2) screen time as predictor of future psychiatric symptoms. Per criterion (b), FT-EC7 only applies when predictors are demographic/contextual only; this paper includes individual psychological/clinical predictors (internalizing, externalizing, attention symptoms). Additionally, the paper reports bidirectional associations, which explicitly excludes FT-EC7 application.
edge_cases_applied: E7 - Phone/SM as outcome in both cross-sectional and longitudinal direction; individual predictor (psychiatric symptoms) involved, so FT-EC7 does not apply
E8 - Multiple inferential tests (logistic regression) with reported odds ratios and confidence intervals, not descriptive only
notes: Paper demonstrates bidirectional associations between psychiatric symptoms and screen time use patterns. Strongest effects observed for nonsocial screen time (YouTube, video games) outcomes compared to social screen time. Study controlled for baseline measures of each, plus demographics (sex, age, race/ethnicity, income).

FT_IC1.evidence: Abstract mentions ABCD dataset but PDF not yet reviewed.

notes: PDF review needed for complete evaluation.
